content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : Feb. 9, 1977


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone Conversation with Charlene Bomar, Assistant City
         Attorney for Modesto.


     In a telephone conversation with Charlene Bomar, a number of
topics were discussed. First, I informed her that a City
Manager who files pursuant to Section 87200 need not file a
disclosure statement pursuant to the Conflict of Interest
Code of the City of Modesto. The City Manager should be
included in the code for purposes of disqualification and
in order to make additional sanctions applicable to him with
respect to disqualification, but he does not have to file
another disclosure statement. Ms. Bomar pointed out that
this can mean that certain lesser officials in the city will
have a broader disclosure obligation than the City Manager
because in addition to disclosing everything required by
Section 87200 (based on the notion that their duties are
broad and indefinable) these persons also disclose manage-
ment positions in certain business entities. On the other
hand, the City Manager does not have to disclose management
positions. I agreed with her that this is anomalous but
pointed out that at present it is the Commission's policy
not to require an additional disclosure statement. This
is based on the provision in Section 87302(b) which states
that any designated employee covered by Article 2 shall not
have to file a disclosure statement under a Code.

     Ms. Bomar and I then discussed the situation of the Planning
Commissioner, which my memorandum of January 28, 1977, also
discusses. I informed Ms. Bomar that the staff is split
with respect to the question of whether the Planning Com-
missioner would have to disqualify himself when there was
not a material financial effect on the Union itself but
there would be a material financial effect on individual
members of the Union. I indicated to her that this raises
a substantial question and that in order to resolve it, it
would be necessary to have a formal opinion request. I
pointed out that the issue would also be involved in

                                                         2-H. 8a

File
P.2
Feb. 9, 1977


situations where you have an association and association
members or the Bar and the individual attorney members of
the Bar. Similar to the situation involving the Union of-
ficer and the individual members of the Union, the issue
will arise of when the Union officer who is serving as a
public official must disqualify himself. In any event, Ms.
Bomar indicated that she would attempt to persuade the City
Attorney and the Planning Commissioner to seek a formal
opinion and I encouraged her to do so.

Finally, we discussed a number of subsidiary issues related
to the correct interpretation of Government Code Sections
81005 and 81009.


KF:jo

State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                          Date : Jan. 28, 1977


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone conversation with David Fitzsimons, Assistant
         City Attorney for the City of Modesto, and Charlene Bomar,
         an attorney in the City Attorney's office.

         On January 27, 1977, I received a call from Assistant City
Attorney Fitzsimons requesting advice with respect to potential
conflicts of interests on the part of a new planning commissioner
in the City of Modesto. Mr. Fitzsimons related the
following facts. The new planning commissioner is the
secretary-treasurer of the Carpenters' Union and also the
secretary-treasurer for the Building Trades Council, which
is an amalgamation of various construction units in the City
of Modesto. The planning commissioner works three-and-a-half
days for the Carpenters' Union and one-and-a-half days for
the Building Trades Council. He is paid by the Carpenters'
Union, but the Building Trades Council reimburses the Carpenters'
Union for the portion of his salary attributable to the one-
and-a-half days that he devotes to the Building Trades Council.
In Modesto, approximately 75% of all commercial construction
is done by unionized labor. On the other hand, almost all of
the residential construction is done by non-union labor.

Mr. Fitzsimons inquired about what types of conflicts the
planning commissioner would have when the planning commission
considered various projects for the City of Modesto, particu-
larly commercial projects that would create work for union
members. I indicated to Mr. Fitzsimons that I felt the
Carpenters' Union and the Building Trades Council were both
sources of income to the planning commissioner and that,
accordingly, potential conflicts did exist with respect to
both of those entities. I also indicated, on the other hand,
that I did not think the individual members of the union or
of the Building Trades Council were sources of income. In
other words, I drew a distinction between the Carpenters'
Union and the Building Trades Council which I believe are
independent entities and the members of those entities; and
I concluded that the sources of income to the planning com-
missioner are the Union and the Council, not the members of
those organizations.

File
P.2
Jan. 28, 1977

Based on this analysis, I then indicated to Mr. Fitzsimons
that a conflict would exist if it were reasonably foreseeable
that any decision of the planning commission could have a
material financial effect on either of the entities. For example, Mr. Fitzsimons indicated that certain projects could
increase the number of union members and hence the amount
of dues collected by the Carpenters' Union. I indicated that
if the increase in income to the Union were material, this
could create a conflict of interest for the planning commissioner.
On the other hand, I indicated that if a decision merely created
more business for union carpenters but did not affect the Carpenters' Union itself, I did not think there would be a prohibited
conflict of interest. I acknowledged that the planning commissioner obviously would be biased in favor of the Union members
but I suggested that this would not be a type of bias which is
prohibited by the Political Reform Act.

Mr. Fitzsimons then provided me with a series of actual examples of issues pending before the planning commission. I discussed these examples in general terms but stated that I would
need more factual information to reach any type of definitive
conclusion with respect to a conflict of interest.
I did, however, attempt to reiterate what I thought were the criteria
that would be involved in deciding whether a conflict existed.
I then encouraged Mr. Fitzsimons to seek a formal opinion and
to provide us with all the necessary facts so that the staff
could review the situations about which he was inquiring. He
indicated that he might provide such an opinion request.

After speaking to Mr. Fitzsimons, I spoke to Ted Prim about
my conversation. Ted indicated that he and Ken Goshorn had
discussed an analogous situation and had concluded that the
members of the Union might be sources of income to the planning
commissioner and that they might disagree with my analysis.
Of course, if Ted is correct, then a decision which merely
benefited the members of the Union, even if it did not benefit
the Union itself, could create a prohibited conflict.

KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: When does a planning commissioner with employment ties to unions and construction organizations face conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding decisions that benefit union members versus the union entities themselves?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Internal memoranda documenting phone conversations rather than formal advice letters. No structured QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Contains preliminary analysis of conflict of interest issues but staff is split on interpretation; no definitive FPPC position given. OCR quality variable with some character recognition errors. Appears to be working notes leading toward potential formal opinion request.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : Feb. 9, 1977 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Ken Finney Subject: Telephone Conversation with Charlene Bomar, Assistant City Attorney for Modesto. In a telephone conversation with Charlene Bomar, a number of topics were discussed. First, I informed her that a City Manager who files pursuant to Section 87200 need not file a disclosure statement pursuant to the Conflict of Interest Code of the City of Modesto. The City Manager should be included in the code for purposes of disqualification and in order to make additional sanctions applicable to him with respect to disqualification, but he does not have to file another disclosure statement. Ms. Bomar pointed out that this can mean that certain lesser officials in the city will have a broader disclosure obligation than the City Manager because in addition to disclosing everything required by Section 87200 (based on the notion that their duties are broad and indefinable) these persons also disclose manage- ment positions in certain business entities. On the other hand, the City Manager does not have to disclose management positions. I agreed with her that this is anomalous but pointed out that at present it is the Commission's policy not to require an additional disclosure statement. This is based on the provision in Section 87302(b) which states that any designated employee covered by Article 2 shall not have to file a disclosure statement under a Code. Ms. Bomar and I then discussed the situation of the Planning Commissioner, which my memorandum of January 28, 1977, also discusses. I informed Ms. Bomar that the staff is split with respect to the question of whether the Planning Com- missioner would have to disqualify himself when there was not a material financial effect on the Union itself but there would be a material financial effect on individual members of the Union. I indicated to her that this raises a substantial question and that in order to resolve it, it would be necessary to have a formal opinion request. I pointed out that the issue would also be involved in 2-H. 8a File P.2 Feb. 9, 1977 situations where you have an association and association members or the Bar and the individual attorney members of the Bar. Similar to the situation involving the Union of- ficer and the individual members of the Union, the issue will arise of when the Union officer who is serving as a public official must disqualify himself. In any event, Ms. Bomar indicated that she would attempt to persuade the City Attorney and the Planning Commissioner to seek a formal opinion and I encouraged her to do so. Finally, we discussed a number of subsidiary issues related to the correct interpretation of Government Code Sections 81005 and 81009. embedding.summary: These are two internal FPPC memoranda from January and February 1977 documenting telephone conversations between FPPC staff (Ken Finney) and Modesto city attorneys regarding conflicts of interest issues, particularly for a planning commissioner who is secretary-treasurer of unions, and broader questions about City Manager disclosure obligations.
